hypotheses: H1: Uniform price auctions generate higher seller revenue than fixed price offerings in IPO settings.
H2: Uniform price auctions result in less frequent and less severe underpricing than fixed price offerings.
H3: Tacit collusion equilibria (TCE) do not arise in uniform price auctions under the experimental conditions.
claim.hypotheses_location: Abstract and Introduction (first sections of the paper)
claim.statement: Uniform price auctions raise more revenue than fixed price offerings in IPOs.
claim.statement_location: Abstract and Introduction (first sections of the paper)

data.source: Laboratory experiment conducted with human subjects (University of Nottingham).
data.wave_or_subset: 20 repeated rounds per session; analysis based on 28 valid groups drawn from 116 participants; data includes bids across rounds (e.g., 280 rounds; 726 U-bids reported).
data.sample_size: 116 participants recruited; results analyzed from 28 valid groups (number of rounds analyzed reported as 280).
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual bids, bidders (participants) and experimental rounds; group-level outcomes (seller revenue per round) for treatment comparisons.
data.notes: Experimental parameters: groups of 4 (3 informed buyers + 1 uninformed); informed buyers receive high/low signals; value of shares = 1 + number of high signals. In UNIFORM treatment: up to six price-quantity bids, market price grid 0 to 6 in increments of 0.01. Quantity limits: informed buyers up to 150,000 units (implicit cost for demands >100,000 units), uninformed buyers up to 80,000 units. Initial endowments differed across treatments to mitigate bankruptcy in UNIFORM. No dataset links or code repository provided in paper.

method.description: Controlled laboratory experiment comparing two treatments: UNIFORM (uniform price auction) and FIXED (fixed price offering). Subjects were organized in groups of four (three informed, one uninformed) and played 20 repeated rounds. Informed subjects received signals (high or low) about value; uninformed subjects received no signal. In UNIFORM, subjects submitted up to six price-quantity bids on a fine price grid; market-clearing price determined where demand met supply and allocations made at the uniform price. In FIXED, bids were at a predetermined price (1.94) with prorated allocation when demand exceeded supply. Behavioral and outcome measures (market price, underpricing, subscription rate, payoffs) were recorded and compared across treatments using nonparametric tests and correlation analyses. Theoretical equilibrium predictions (TCE, EH, EHLU) from the Biais and Faugeron-Grouzet framework were used to interpret behaviors.
steps: Recruit participants and form groups of four (3 informed, 1 uninformed) maintaining same composition through the session.
Provide informed subjects with signals indicating high or low value; uninformed subjects receive no signal.
Set treatment parameters: UNIFORM (price grid 0–6 by 0.01, up to six price-quantity bids, quantity limits and implicit costs) and FIXED (fixed price 1.94, prorated allocation rule).
Give initial endowments (note: UNIFORM initial endowments higher to reduce bankruptcy risk).
Run 20 repeated rounds, collecting all bids and allocation outcomes each round.
For UNIFORM, determine market-clearing uniform price each round and allocate shares accordingly; for FIXED, allocate prorated quantities if oversubscribed.
Compute outcome metrics per round: market price, subscription rate, seller revenue, underpricing, bidders' payoffs.
Perform statistical analyses: compare treatments (e.g., Wilcoxon signed-ranks test), compute Spearman rank-order correlations, and test significance of differences and correlations.
Compare empirical outcomes to theoretical equilibrium predictions (TCE, EH, EHLU) and interpret bidder behavior patterns.
method.models: Descriptive and inferential analyses comparing treatments; nonparametric tests (Wilcoxon signed-ranks), Spearman rank-order correlations; interpretation against theoretical auction equilibrium models from the Biais & Faugeron-Grouzet framework (TCE, EH, EHLU). No explicit econometric regression frameworks were reported in the summary.
method.outcome_variable: Seller revenue/market-clearing price, subscription rate (full subscription), underpricing (price relative to market value), bidders' payoffs.
method.independent_variables: Treatment type (UNIFORM vs FIXED), number of high-value signals in a round (market value), bidder type (H, L, U), round number (to capture dynamics over repetitions).
method.control_variables: Group composition held constant across rounds; experimental parameters (price grid, quantity caps, initial endowments) set by treatment. No additional observational controls reported in summary.

results.summary: Uniform price auctions produced higher market prices and greater seller revenue than fixed price offerings in the laboratory experiments. UNIFORM exhibited less frequent and less severe underpricing relative to FIXED. There was no empirical evidence of tacit collusion equilibria (TCE); instead behaviors aligned more closely with alternative equilibria (EHLU/EH) where high-signal bidders bid more aggressively, increasing prices and seller revenue. UNIFORM achieved a higher subscription rate and higher average prices across rounds, although UNIFORM's average price did not significantly exceed the unconditional expected market value. Results were supported by nonparametric tests and rank correlations.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Average market price difference (UNIFORM vs FIXED)
Correlation between market price and market value (UNIFORM)
UNIFORM market price vs unconditional expected market value (2.5)
UNIFORM market price vs expected value for L bidder (2)
Proportion of bids conforming to symmetric collusive quantity (TCE)
H bidders bid more aggressively than L and U bidders
numerical_results.unit: relative bidding behavior (test statistic not stated)
